speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor Rosalie Steinweis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And we were talking at the end of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What started to happen then?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning of 1944, we-- our camp received the transport of Hungarian women who came to us wearing just a pair of shoes and a dress, nothing at-- else at all, no, underwear, no coats, no nothing. And their heads were shaved. And that was a time when Hungary was actually an ally of Germany for a while. But then Germany occupied them. And they deported the Jewish people. And the funny thing is that some of these women, were either they were converted to Christianity and some of them were ultra-orthodox. And they were brought out camp. And they told us what had happened to them. And then-- I think it must've been March of 1944-- when one night, Mengele and two other probably doctors, too, or officers came to our building, to our-- to our factory, the-- actually to our camp. And they locate-- they locate themselves in the office of our secretary. They drew a circle in chalk. It must've been like, oh, 30 inches in diameter. And they made us strip all the way. And we all had to step into that circle and turn front and back to-- to these three people. And after seeing us, we were given a number on a string. I received a number which was a little disk on a-- on a string-- 21,173, that was the number that I received-- and dismissed. Once you received a number, you kind of felt at ease. Because some people didn't. And those people were-- were set to be ready to-- to be deported to Auschwitz. Of course, there was plenty crying and screaming. Because there was a mother and a daughter where the daughter received a number and the mother didn't. Or, there were two sisters when one received a number and the other didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew that the number meant that you weren't going to Auschwitz? You knew that--

speaker: interviewee
text: The number meant that you were going-- for the time being, that you were going to live. My sister and I, we both received numbers. So hers was the next after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction? Did you have any interaction with Dr. Mengele at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever. He didn't even speak. He didn't even speak to anybody. They set up-- he was sitting in a chair on one side. The other two were sitting-- he was sitting on the one wall. They were sitting on the other side, so as to have a good view of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't-- we didn't hear them speak a word. It was just point of a finger, you know? So I suppose we-- I didn't even know what he-- what the pointing meant until I received the number. How did you know who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we found out later that that was him. We didn't know then. In fact, we didn't even know that such a man existed. Because we had no information in camp. You know, we didn't know anything what was going on in the outside world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to look at his face? Did he look at your face? Or, was it even like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't look directly at him. I don't even think that they looked directly at our faces. They looked at our bodies, what condition they were in. I-- my normal weight is now about 118, 120. At that time, I weighed 90 pounds. And my sister, who was taller than I, weighed 80 pounds. She was like skin and bones. I am short, and maybe that's why, it's-- it's-- you know, my weight was better than hers. And somehow that starvation diet that we received maybe somehow allowed us to live and function. But some people looked even worse than that. So after-- after that, after that evening, we returned to work. And-- and we worked. And we also that time-- no, that wasn't 1944 yet. That much came later. But 1944 was more or less an uneventful year. We went to work. We came back from work. And the Hungarian girls also went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear from them, you know, these women who came in wearing very little with their shaved heads?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of them didn't speak Jewish. Of course, they didn't speak Polish. And we didn't speak Hungarian. We-- there was very little communication. In fact, they-- they did not get accommodated in the same building where we were. Because they-- this was-- that was once an old factory. It was once a factory which was later on left alone. And it was a few buildings. They-- they were located in a different building. Later on, we noticed these women. Their hair started growing back. And a lot of them were very intelligent. They were educated. Some of them spoke English, but we didn't know English either. But they were very intelligent women. They-- I remember, there was one. Her name was Lily Schwartz. She was a singer. And she would sing at night. She sang so beautiful. And you know, and then the year was going on. Then like towards the end of year, we didn't know-- you know, sometimes we would-- I remember there once was an air raid. We never had any air raids. But that particular time, we had an air raid. And we were, at the time, at work. And I remember that there was snow and rain. It must've been the late fall or the early winter of 1943. And there was one of these guards, these SS women who used to work once with us. She had epilepsy. Because during that air raid, we were running. They did not take us to a shelter. We remained in the factory. But all the Nazi people went into a shelter. And she had one of her epileptic seizures. And she dropped right in the mud and dirt. And we were-- we felt good about it. Anything bad that happened to them made us feel better. Anyway, that was about the only air raid at the time. And it passed quickly. Then towards the end of the year, the beginning of 1945, we found out about the death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we found-- find out? Because we were located when they were running-- when they were bringing people from the west to the east, and then people from the east to the west, we were like in the center. So one transport stopped at our camp overnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we weren't allowed to go to the window to look. In fact, one of the women-- we were-- that time, we were located on the third floor of the factory. And one woman-- there were these little windows in the large one, these little tiny windows that you open as part of a large window. A woman stuck her head out to see what's going on. And she was shot dead right then and there. And these people being [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chased back and forth. And one morning, a few girls, including me, were sent to the railroad station to clean up after the people were stopped there at the train. And we saw a lot of blood there. And once on the way from work, from the factory-- no, from the camp to the factory, we had to march under an overpass. And we saw a transport on a train which was being also sent from, I think it was, from west to east. Because both fronts approached about the same time, the Russian or one side and the Allies on the other side. And we saw these people bunched up, standing up in an open cargo train on top of the other. And they were stiff. Most of them were probably dead at the time. And we saw that.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were prisoners or soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Jews. They were people who were being sent from one place to another. Instead of being marched, these people were sent by train. And this is the extent that we saw of-- of the atrocities that were happening. Because at our place, we used to get an occasional slap, an occasional scolding. And, but no really-- also, sending away people to Auschwitz. In fact, there was a time-- I don't remember what time it was, end of 1943. They gathered a lot of people who were brought from all various area camps, who were going to go to Auschwitz for destruction. These people were unable to work anymore. So at-- at our camp was the gathering point. And from there, they sent them to Auschwitz. And if somebody of our people was also disabled, they were included in that transport. That's why there was so much despair after the selection. When we were given numbers and the others weren't, they already knew what would happen if they don't receive a number. Anyway, then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we concluded that maybe the war is coming to an end. And the Germans-- we didn't know about Stalingrad. We didn't know about the Western front, the Eastern front. We just knew that something is up and-- you know, from the shifting of the people in both directions. And then, it must have been like February or so, we didn't go to work anymore. Because they weren't able to receive fuel to run the factory. So they gave us some jobs around, extra cleanup jobs, extra things up, extra doing things around the camp. And we didn't even have enough fuel to cook our food. So we used to chop up some old bunks and whatever was possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and those that didn't do any work didn't receive any food. Somehow we managed, between my sister and I, that one of us was always given work. So between the two of us, we could share the food. And then around March, they took us to-- to dig the-- how do you call this? On the front line? Trenches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trenches, right. They took us to dig trenches in the woods. This was a beautiful part of the country, what was a mountainous part of the country. And they-- they took us to dig trenches. And we also received food if we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we dug the trenches, but nothing happened. We could see an occasional plane. There were no air raids at the time. But once, I think it must've been already April, middle of April.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I found a leaflet that was dropped from a plane. I found a leaflet in the wood. And that time was already President Truman. That must have-- so we knew, excuse me, we knew about President Roosevelt from before the war. By that time, it was already President Truman in-- in the White House. And they-- they were throwing down leaflets to the civilian population that they should lay down their arms, that they should not support the Nazis anymore, that the war is coming to an end. And I found that leaflet. Of course, I didn't-- it was written in German. So, you know, we knew what it was about. But I was afraid to show it to anybody, because we were being guarded by the SS with rifles.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't tell your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my sister, yes. But we decided between us that we were not going to show it to anybody. Because you-- you never know, somebody could tell on you. You know? Because sad as it is, in a lot of men concentration camps or forced labor camps, there were like Jewish collaborators. Not collaborators to-- to help the Nazis win the war, but to ingratiate themselves with the Germans. They-- there used to even be Jewish kapos that used to beat up on Jews, sad as it is. Anyway, that lasted another couple of weeks. And then, I'll never remember, that was the last Saturday. One of these guards, was-- he didn't talk. There weren't too many of us. We must have been maybe 50 or 60 women digging the trenches. And there were only three people guarding us. Of course, we had no place to go-- and with rifles. The two others used to talk to each other. And that one, he must've been a sergeant. He didn't even talk to them. But then it was Saturday. That was from later on, we knew that it was the last Saturday before the end of the war. He started talking to us. He said-- it was raining that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they had like a huge gazebo in that place. I suppose they had it for-- for picnics, you know. And so people used to hide there during the rain. So he allowed us to stop digging and go into that gazebo.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started talking to us. And he said, you know, the war is coming to an end. And we are losing. And now, you are going to be doing to us what we are doing to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was nothing we could say. We were just happy that we hear this-- this type of news. So this was on a Saturday. And that must've been like the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Wednesday was the 8th of May. So that could've been like the 5th of May or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, when we-- when we came back to the-- to the camp, the woman commandant, she was running around like crazy. And then one-- and then she came into the-- into the camp. And she was crying. And she says, the Fuhrer is [NON-ENGLISH]. Meaning, the Fuhrer is dead. In another few days, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Russians drove up. The gates opened. Actually, the gates opened the day before. But the Germans were running. We opened the gates ourselves. There was nobody to guard us. Everybody ran away. And the next day is when the Russians came in, which was May 8. They came in with tanks and trucks and-- and accordions and music and singing. And they gave us food. And-- and that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, after about a week-- oh, yes, of course, there wasn't much food left in camp. Whatever there was, we had-- we had eaten. And so we went to-- to town to the railroad station. And we went out to the villages to buy some food from the peasants.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave us some food. Yes, they gave us some bread. They gave us some eggs. But then we decided it was time to leave. And we started on the way back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all, we went into-- no, there was no money. And you didn't need money, because everybody was going home. And the trains were operating under limited capacity. And we filled up the trains wherever we could. And we went back to Poland. It took us a couple of days to get back to Poland. In the meantime, we met people on the train from the same town. And we came back to Poland. And somebody-- the one woman that we met from our town-- took us in to her house. And, of course, when we returned to our town, the next morning, my sister and I went to City Hall. They should assign us a place to live. Of course, a lot of Germans fled. You know, they fled with the German army. After the Russians were approaching, they-- so there was enough place for us to-- to get an apartment. We-- there was my sister, myself, and three more girls. We shared an apartment. And we lived there for a while. There was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try checking on your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no way to find out. But then I met people. I met people who told me-- in this particular case, I met my husband, who told me about the time when we were at concentration camp. During the time where we were still able to receive mail, my mother went to work on the coal mine. So did he, my husband. And at that time, they were loading coal on those little wagons, which were attached to a rail. And my mother had an accident. A-- a little wagon turned over on her and tore off her leg.

speaker: interviewee
text: So naturally, she was sent to Auschwitz. But my father never let us know about it. He always said, mother is OK and so are the children. He never lets us know about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I found out, also from my husband, that my father was sent to Markstadt. And he became ill. And he was already in his early 50s. And he was also sent to Auschwitz for extermination. And we found out from other people who came back that the two younger children who-- you see, the way that they work it was that people who were able to work were sent to work. People who were too old to work were exterminated. The same thing for people who were too young to work. But at that time when the two younger children, they would have probably been able to go to work. But by that time, the tide was already turning against Germany. So the only way was to, you know, get rid of the Jews in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yeah, you met up with your husband at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I met my husband in Poland, 1945. And we started going out together. And then we went to-- Russia was occupying Poland. We didn't know how long this is going to take. But we decided to leave Poland. Since my mother's entire family lived in the United States, we decide to leave Poland and go to Germany where the majority of-- of liberated Jews were all located. And maybe from there, we could continue further out. By that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know yet-- my sister and I didn't know yet-- whether our other brother was alive, the one who went-- was taken away together with us. But then, on the way to Germany, we stopped in Prague, Czechoslovakia. And at that time, we found out from somebody that my brother is alive. So we like crossed ourselves while traveling. We were going to Germany. And my brother went back to Poland looking for us. My sister remained in Poland at that time. And then my brother brought my sister. We were, you know, later on by word-of-mouth, by letters, we were able to trace each other. And then my brother and my sister came to Germany, Deggendorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: The DP camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like at the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was kind of a carefree life. There was nothing doing. We got married there in the DP camp. In fact, our wedding, our religious ceremony, was officiated by-- by an American army chaplain. Yes, because, you know, there was-- couldn't find a rabbi. And there was-- there was army stationed everywhere in Germany. So his name was Rabbi [INAUDIBLE]. He was wearing the uniform and the helmet with the two [INAUDIBLE]. And, in fact, the best men was also an American army captain whose name was Captain [INAUDIBLE]. I remember. You know, sometimes you don't remember things. But then when you start talking about it, it just comes back. And our reception [LAUGHS] was the food from the army rations. Yeah, we opened a lot of cans. And-- and that's that. And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in a DP camp for how long?

words.text: a DP camp for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the DP camp from 1945-- September, August, something like that. And our son was born. We got married on October 10, 1945. Our first son was born April 10-- beg your pardon, April 9. I get confused, my husband was born April 10-- April 9, 1947. In the meantime, we were able to communicate with our relatives in the United States. Funny thing, I remember the address of one  of my aunts. And it's stayed in my memory all through these years. Somehow I-- I made sure that it became ingrained in my memory, so I could, if we survive, that we should get be able to get in touch with them. And 1947 when my son was born, they sent us packages and things like that. And then we already-- we were able to apply for immigration to the United States. Our family sent us papers. But when my son was three months old, we left Deggendorf. And we were-- we were gathered with a lot of other people. And it was such a temporary camp where we went through the process of being examined by doctors and-- and-- for immigration, American doctors. We had to undergo extensive medical examinations. In fact, even my three month old baby was examined.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we weren't-- we had to wait for a visa. So we came here under the DP camp, under the Displaced Persons Act that President Truman initiated. And so we, between the time that we were able to-- to go, we stayed with somebody, a cousin of ours in-- in one of the small towns in Germany. And then, in November of 1947, we were given the departure date. We had to gather in one place in Munich. And from there, we went to Bremerhaven. And there we were loaded on a ship called Ernie Pyle. I have a picture of the ship. And we arrived in the United States on the 29th of November, 1947, which was the day when the United States-- the United Nations approved the Partition of Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to the US, you stayed with relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We stayed with-- first we stayed with one aunt in Brooklyn. And then-- my-- my mother was one of seven children. She was the only one remaining in Poland. When we arrived here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop right here, actually. It's a good place to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. About my-- my--
